frames.caption: Seed missing = nums.Length = 3. Indices run 0..2 but the universe is 0..3, so 3 has no array slot to cancel it.
frames.note: Seed missing with n = nums.Length itself. The array has slots for indices 0..n-1 but the universe is 0..n, so index n has NO matching array slot — it can never be cancelled by a value. Folding it in up front is what lets the survivor be a real candidate: if n is the missing number, it stays; otherwise the genuine value n cancels it later.

frames.caption: i = 0: pick index 0 and its value nums[0] = 3. We are about to fold BOTH into missing.
frames.note: XOR is self-inverse (a ^ a = 0), commutative, and associative, so order does not matter and we can fold the whole stream into one accumulator. At slot i we XOR in BOTH the index i and the value nums[i]. Across the full pass every number 0..n that is present contributes its bit pattern an EVEN number of times (once as some index, once as some value) and cancels to 0 — except the missing one, which appears only as an index.

frames.caption: missing ^= 0 ^ 3  ->  3 ^ 0 ^ 3 = 0.
frames.note: XOR is self-inverse (a ^ a = 0), commutative, and associative, so order does not matter and we can fold the whole stream into one accumulator. At slot i we XOR in BOTH the index i and the value nums[i]. Across the full pass every number 0..n that is present contributes its bit pattern an EVEN number of times (once as some index, once as some value) and cancels to 0 — except the missing one, which appears only as an index.

frames.caption: i = 1: pick index 1 and its value nums[1] = 0. We are about to fold BOTH into missing.
frames.note: XOR is self-inverse (a ^ a = 0), commutative, and associative, so order does not matter and we can fold the whole stream into one accumulator. At slot i we XOR in BOTH the index i and the value nums[i]. Across the full pass every number 0..n that is present contributes its bit pattern an EVEN number of times (once as some index, once as some value) and cancels to 0 — except the missing one, which appears only as an index.

frames.caption: missing ^= 1 ^ 0  ->  0 ^ 1 ^ 0 = 1.
frames.note: XOR is self-inverse (a ^ a = 0), commutative, and associative, so order does not matter and we can fold the whole stream into one accumulator. At slot i we XOR in BOTH the index i and the value nums[i]. Across the full pass every number 0..n that is present contributes its bit pattern an EVEN number of times (once as some index, once as some value) and cancels to 0 — except the missing one, which appears only as an index.

frames.caption: i = 2: pick index 2 and its value nums[2] = 1. We are about to fold BOTH into missing.
frames.note: XOR is self-inverse (a ^ a = 0), commutative, and associative, so order does not matter and we can fold the whole stream into one accumulator. At slot i we XOR in BOTH the index i and the value nums[i]. Across the full pass every number 0..n that is present contributes its bit pattern an EVEN number of times (once as some index, once as some value) and cancels to 0 — except the missing one, which appears only as an index.

frames.caption: missing ^= 2 ^ 1  ->  1 ^ 2 ^ 1 = 2.
frames.note: XOR is self-inverse (a ^ a = 0), commutative, and associative, so order does not matter and we can fold the whole stream into one accumulator. At slot i we XOR in BOTH the index i and the value nums[i]. Across the full pass every number 0..n that is present contributes its bit pattern an EVEN number of times (once as some index, once as some value) and cancels to 0 — except the missing one, which appears only as an index.

frames.caption: Loop done. Every present number cancelled against its index; the lone survivor is the missing number — return 2.
frames.note: After the fold, every present number has paired off with itself and vanished; the one index that never met a matching value is left standing in the accumulator. That is the missing number. Unlike the Gauss-sum formula n*(n+1)/2 - sum, this never overflows because XOR stays within the word width no matter how large n grows — its main practical edge.
